title: Hay-Meaken. Nunchen Time
content:
A.
Back here, but now, the jobber John
Come by, an' cried, "Well done, zing on,
I thought as I come down the hill,
An' heï¿½rd your zongs a-ringï¿½n sh'ill,
Who woudden like to come, an' fling
A peï¿½ir o' prongs where you did zing?"
J.
Aye, aye, he woudden vind it plaï¿½,
To work all day a-meï¿½kï¿½n hay,
Or pitchï¿½n o't, to eï¿½rms a-spread
By lwoaders, yards above his head,
'T'ud meï¿½ke en wipe his drippï¿½n brow.
A.
Or else a-reï¿½kï¿½n a'ter plow.
J.
Or workï¿½n, wi' his nimble pick,
A-stiffled wi' the hay, at rick.
A.
Our Company would suit en best,
When we do teï¿½ke our bit o' rest,
At nunch, a-gather'd here below
The sheï¿½de theï¿½se wide-bough'd woak do drow,
Where hissï¿½n froth mid rise, an' float
In horns o' eï¿½le, to wet his droat.
J.
Aye, if his swellï¿½n han' could drag
A meat-slice vrom his dinner bag.
'T'ud meï¿½ke the busy little chap
Look rather glum, to zee his lap
Wi' all his meal ov woone dry crowst,
An' vinny cheese so dry as dowst.
A.
Well, I dont grumble at my food,
'Tis wholesome, John, an' zoo 'tis good.
J.
Whose reï¿½ke is that a-lyï¿½n there?
Do look a bit the woo'se vor wear.
A.
Oh! I mus' get the man to meï¿½ke
A tooth or two vor thik wold reï¿½ke,
'Tis leï¿½bor lost to strike a stroke
Wi' him, wi' ha'f his teeth a-broke.
J.
I should ha' thought your han' too fine
To break your reï¿½ke, if I broke mine.
A.
The ramsclaws thin'd his wooden gum
O' two teeth here, an' here were zome
That broke off when I reï¿½k'd a patch
O' groun' wi' Jimmy, vor a match:
An' here's a gap where woone or two
Wer broke by Simon's clumsy shoe,
An' when I gi'ed his poll a poke,
Vor better luck, another broke.
In what a veag have you a-swung
Your pick, though, John? His stem's a-sprung.
J.
When I an' Simon had a het
O' pookï¿½n, yonder, vor a bet,
The prongs o'n gi'd a tump a poke,
An' then I vound the stem o'n broke,
But they do meï¿½ke the stems o' picks
O' stuff so brittle as a kicks.
A.
There's poor wold Jeï¿½ne, wi' wrinkled skin,
A-tellï¿½n, wi' her peakï¿½d chin,
Zome teï¿½le ov her young days, poor soul.
Do meï¿½ke the young-woones smile. 'Tis droll.
What is it? Stop, an' let's goo near.
I do like theï¿½se wold teï¿½les. Let's hear.
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Barnes represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of William Barnes's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
